record_office_terminology.land_records: Clerk of Circuit Court (deeds, mortgages, plats, liens)
record_office_terminology.probate: Clerk of Circuit Court (wills, estates, guardianships, inventories)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Clerk of Circuit Court (county-level marriage licenses; statewide recording from 1927)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1917 (births/deaths statewide registration; county-level records often earlier; delayed birth certificates common)

burned_county_authority: Florida State Archives, Guide to County Records; WPA Historical Records Survey — Florida; FamilySearch Research Wiki — Florida Burned Counties; Florida Historical Society — County Records Guide
administrative_notes: Florida was Spanish territory from 1513 to 1821 (with a British period 1763-1783), became a US territory in 1822, and achieved statehood on March 3, 1845 (27th state). The state has 67 counties. The two original territorial counties were St. Johns (from Spanish East Florida) and Escambia (from Spanish West Florida), both created in 1821. Spanish colonial records are held at the P.K. Yonge Library of Florida History (University of Florida, Gainesville), St. Augustine Historical Society, and the Archivo General de Indias (Seville, Spain). The Seminole Wars (1817-1818, 1835-1842, 1855-1858) profoundly shaped settlement patterns and record creation in central and south Florida. Many south Florida counties were formed in the 20th century as Everglades drainage opened land for settlement. The Clerk of Circuit Court holds county records in Florida. The Florida State Archives in Tallahassee is the primary state repository.

core_repositories.repository: Florida State Archives, R.A. Gray Building, Tallahassee
core_repositories.description: Florida's primary state repository. Houses territorial and state government records, county record microfilm, Spanish colonial transcriptions, Seminole Wars records, Civil War records, vital records, military records, land records, newspapers, photographs, and maps. The Florida Memory digital collections provide online access to many documents. Florida Genealogy collection includes county marriage records, military pension files, and WPA records.

core_repositories.source_name: P.K. Yonge Library of Florida History (University of Florida)
core_repositories.repository: P.K. Yonge Library of Florida History, George A. Smathers Libraries, University of Florida, Gainesville
core_repositories.description: Premier research library for Florida history. Houses Spanish colonial records (transcriptions and translations from Archivo General de Indias), British period documents, territorial-era records, maps, photographs, and manuscripts. The University of Florida Digital Collections include many digitized Florida materials. Essential for research predating US acquisition (1821).

core_repositories.repository: St. Augustine Historical Society Research Library, St. Augustine
core_repositories.description: Major repository for the oldest city in the US. Holds Spanish colonial records, British period documents, parish registers, maps, photographs, and family papers. Cathedral Parish (Catholic) records from 1594. Essential for East Florida research. Collections span 1565 to present.

core_repositories.repository: Florida Historical Society Library of Florida History, Cocoa
core_repositories.description: Statewide historical society since 1856. Publishes the Florida Historical Quarterly (since 1908). Library holds manuscripts, rare books, maps, and genealogical resources. Comprehensive index to the Florida Historical Quarterly provides valuable research leads.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections: county records (deeds, marriages, probate), vital records, cemetery records, military records, and church records. Microfilm partnership with Florida State Archives provides digitized county records for many Florida counties. Spanish colonial Catholic parish records for St. Augustine included.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability, burned county information, and links to digitized microfilm. Includes guides for Spanish colonial records and Seminole Wars era research.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Florida newspapers from the territorial era forward. Includes East Florida Herald (St. Augustine), Florida Times-Union (Jacksonville), Pensacola Gazette, and county weeklies across the state. Legal notices, estate advertisements, and community news.

University of Florida's digitized Florida newspaper collection. Includes many papers not in Chronicling America. Strong coverage of county weeklies, ethnic newspapers, and African American newspapers. Florida Historical Quarterly index provides article-level access to scholarly research.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Florida State Archives — Enslaved Persons and African American Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Personal property tax rolls listing enslaved persons by slaveholder, estate inventories, bills of sale, plantation records, and court records involving enslaved persons. Florida was a slave state from territorial period through 1865. The plantation belt in North Florida (Gadsden, Jefferson, Leon, Jackson, Madison) had the highest enslaved populations. Freedmen's Bureau records for Florida at NARA/FamilySearch.

Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands for Florida (1865-1872). Labor contracts, marriage registrations, school reports, and complaint files. NARA microfilm M1869. Many indexed on FamilySearch. Florida's Freedmen's Bureau operated offices in Jacksonville, Tallahassee, and other towns.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Black Archives Research Center and Museum (FAMU)
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Major repository for African American history in Florida. Manuscript collections, photographs, oral histories, and community records. FAMU (founded 1887) archives document historically Black college history and the African American experience across Florida.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Seminole Wars Records — Florida State Archives / NARA
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Florida State Archives, Tallahassee / NARA, Washington DC
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records of the three Seminole Wars (First 1817-1818, Second 1835-1842, Third 1855-1858) including military correspondence, muster rolls, Indian removal records, and treaties. NARA Record Group 75 (Bureau of Indian Affairs) and RG 94 (Adjutant General's Office). Florida State Archives has territorial governor's correspondence regarding Indian affairs. The Dade Massacre (1835) and other battles documented in military records.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Seminole Tribe of Florida and Miccosukee Tribe Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Seminole Tribe of Florida, Ah-Tah-Thi-Ki Museum, Big Cypress / Miccosukee Tribe
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Seminole people who remained in Florida (never surrendered/removed) maintain tribal records at the Ah-Tah-Thi-Ki Museum on the Big Cypress Reservation. Tribal enrollment records, oral histories, and cultural documentation. The Miccosukee Tribe of Indians of Florida maintains separate tribal records. Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) records at NARA document federal interactions with both tribes.

military_records.description: Confederate pension applications (Florida pensions from 1885), Confederate service records, Seminole Wars militia muster rolls, Spanish-American War records (Tampa was the embarkation point), WWI and WWII draft registration cards, and Florida National Guard records. Confederate pension files include family details, residence history, and unit information. Florida Memory digital collections include some military records.

Federal military records including Seminole Wars records (RG 94, RG 393), Civil War Confederate and Union records, Fort Brooke (Tampa), Fort Marion (St. Augustine), Fort Pickens (Pensacola), and other Florida military installation records. Compiled military service records for Florida Confederate soldiers. Pension files for Union veterans from Florida.

land_and_property.source_name: Spanish Land Grants — Florida State Archives / Bureau of Land Management
land_and_property.repository: Florida State Archives, Tallahassee / BLM General Land Office
land_and_property.description: Spanish land grants (confirmed and unconfirmed) from the First Spanish Period (1565-1763), British Period (1763-1783), and Second Spanish Period (1783-1821). After US acquisition (1821), the US confirmed many Spanish grants. Bureau of Land Management General Land Office Records include Florida homestead patents, cash entries, and military bounty land warrants. Florida State Archives holds the Florida Board of Trustees land records.

land_and_property.source_name: Florida Department of Environmental Protection — State Lands Records
land_and_property.description: Swamp and Overflowed Lands records (1850+), Internal Improvement Fund records, and Everglades drainage records. These are essential for understanding 19th and 20th century land disposition in Florida, particularly in south Florida where drainage created new land for settlement.

vital_records.source_name: Florida Department of Health — Bureau of Vital Statistics
vital_records.description: State vital records: births from 1917 (some county and city registration earlier — Jacksonville from 1865, Key West from 1870s), deaths from 1917. Marriage records from 1927 at state level (county-level earlier). Historical birth and death records at Florida State Archives. Pre-1917 vital events must be found in church records, county records, newspaper notices, or cemetery records.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Catholic Parish Records — Diocese of St. Augustine / Archdiocese of Miami
church_and_cemetery.repository: Cathedral Parish, St. Augustine / Various diocesan archives
church_and_cemetery.description: Catholic parish registers from the Spanish colonial period. Cathedral Parish (St. Augustine) records begin 1594, making them among the oldest church records in the US. Baptism, marriage, and burial records for the Spanish, British, and American periods. Pensacola Catholic records from the 1690s. Diocese of St. Augustine archives hold records for many north and central Florida parishes.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Florida. Coverage varies by county — older north Florida and St. Augustine cemeteries have extensive transcriptions. Pioneer cemeteries in rural areas are important for early families. Florida Master Site File at the Division of Historical Resources records cemetery locations.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes the Florida Genealogical Society Journal. Maintains research resources, sponsors conferences, and coordinates with county genealogical societies across the state.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, county histories, and research guides for all 67 Florida counties.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Florida has several extinct or renamed counties. Mosquito County (created 1824 from St. Johns) was renamed Orange County in 1845 — early records filed under both names at Florida State Archives. New River County (created 1858 from Columbia) was renamed Bradford County in 1861 — records continuous under both names. Fayette County was briefly proposed but never organized. St. Lucie County was originally created in 1844 but abolished in 1855, with its territory going to Brevard; a new St. Lucie County was created in 1905 from Brevard — the two St. Lucie Counties are different jurisdictions. Benton County (created 1843) was renamed Hernando County in 1850. Dade County (created 1836) was renamed Miami-Dade County in 1997. These name changes can cause confusion when searching early Florida records.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Florida's courthouse record losses are primarily from fires in the 19th century and hurricane damage. The most significant losses: Jackson County (1849 fire — most early records destroyed, with additional Civil War-era losses), Hamilton County (1895 fire — many early records destroyed), Santa Rosa County (1869 fire — many early records destroyed), Walton County (1885 fire — early records destroyed), Washington County (1890 fire — many early records destroyed), and Monroe County (1886 Key West fire and hurricane damage). For burned counties, the Florida State Archives maintains some surviving state copies of tax rolls, governor's correspondence, and land records. Spanish colonial records at P.K. Yonge Library and St. Augustine Historical Society predate most county formation and provide alternative documentation for territorial-era research.
